architecture_fit.note: Not a transformer/attention mechanism — it recomputes the evaluator's own transitive closure and returns reach^T as 'attention', sidestepping the goal's actual question about what attention can express.

baseline_comparison.note: Relies on the task's built-in uniform baseline (0.5, lift +0.5) rather than constructing its own strawman/ablation.

faithfulness.note: Purely synthetic hand-built fn with no model; README does not explicitly flag the absence of causal evidence nor propose what an ablation would look like.

operating_range.note: Sweeps 4 densities (0.1–0.5) all at 1.0, but N fixed at 8 and the exact algorithm never reveals a breaking point — no edge degradation shown.

hardcoded_weights_bonus.note: Genuinely hand-built, zero-training circuit (Floyd-Warshall closure) that exactly reproduces the target order, though it recomputes ground truth rather than expressing it through attention.

visual_judgement.note: Functional ASCII-markdown heatmap with ancestor stars, but only one matrix is shown — README's claim of 'three aligned heatmaps' (adjacency/closure/attention) is not actually rendered.

visualisation_rationale.note: Rationale (descendant rows light up on ancestor columns) is sensible but overstated relative to the single text heatmap actually built.

notes: The attempt achieves a perfect topo_robustness of 1.0 by hand-building an exact transitive-closure circuit on GPU, but it bypasses the goal's real question: it recomputes the evaluator's own ancestor relation and returns it as 'attention' rather than showing an attention mechanism encoding the partial order. It is a clean, deterministic hand-built solution (strong on the hardcoded-weights axis) yet weak on architecture fit and faithfulness, and the visualisation underdelivers relative to the README. Mean of scored items places it borderline.
